title: The Geate A-vallen To
content:
In the zunsheen of our zummers
Wiï¿½ the hay time now a-come,
How busy wer we out a-vield
Wiï¿½ vew a-left at hwome,
When waggons rumbled out ov yard
Red wheeled, wiï¿½ body blue,
And back behind ï¿½em loudly slammï¿½d
The geate aï¿½vallen to.

Drough daysheen ov how many years
The geate haï¿½ now a-swung
Behind the veet oï¿½ vull-grown men
And vootsteps of the young.
Drough years oï¿½ days it swung to us
Behind each little shoe,
As we tripped lightly on avore
The geate a-vallen to.

In evenen time oï¿½ starry night
How mother zot at hwome,
And kept her bleazen vier bright
Till father should ha? come,
Anï¿½ how she quickenï¿½d up and smiled
Anï¿½ stirred her vier anew,
To hear the trampen hoï¿½sesï¿½ steps
Anï¿½ geate a-vallen to.

There?s moon-sheen now in nights oï¿½ fall
When leaves be brown vrom green,
When, to the slammen oï¿½ the geate,
Our Jennyï¿½s ears be keen,
When the wold dog do wag his tail,
Anï¿½ Jean could tell to who,
As he do come in drough the geate,
The geate a-vallen to.

Anï¿½ oft do come a saddened hour
When there must goo away
One well-beloved to our heartï¿½s core,
Vor long, perhaps vor aye:
Anï¿½ oh! it is a touchen thing
The loven heart must rue,
To hear behind his last farewell
The geate a-vallen to.
meaning:
William Barnes's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "The Geate A-vallen To"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
